results.title: Malicious User can call lockOnBehalf repeatedly extend a users unlockTime, removing their ability to withdraw previously locked tokens
results.reasoning: Yes. Issue #6 in the audit explicitly identifies that lockOnBehalf allows anyone to reset a recipient’s unlockTime via _lock’s unconditional assignment (lockedToken.unlockTime = block.timestamp + _lockDuration), including with zero/near-zero quantity, and that repeated calls can indefinitely prevent withdrawal. This is the same mechanism, code path, and impact as the described vulnerability. The same fixes (restricting lockOnBehalf, requiring consent, or not overwriting unlockTime) would address both. While the description also notes griefing of setLockDuration, that arises from the same root cause and would be resolved by the same fix.

results.title: Invalid validation allows users to unlock early
results.reasoning: Yes. Audit issue #5 describes the exact same flaw in setLockDuration: the validation uses block.timestamp + _duration while the assignment uses lastLockTime + _duration, allowing users to reduce unlockTime (even into the past) and withdraw early. It is the same function, same code path, same root cause, and would be fixed by the same correction (validating against lastLockTime + _duration).
